title: Splitting the Motion: Two Simple Problems in Disguise

beats.say: Here's the key insight physicists use to crack projectile motion: horizontal and vertical don't talk to each other. Gravity only pulls down — it never touches the horizontal speed. That means we can separate them completely.

card.content: **Independence of motion:** Horizontal and vertical components evolve independently. Gravity acts only on the vertical axis.

beats.say: Watch the single launch arrow split into two. The blue arrow goes right — that's the horizontal component. The red arrow goes straight up — that's the vertical. Together, they form a perfect rectangle, and the original arrow is exactly their diagonal sum.

At $45°$, cosine and sine are both $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, so each component is $v_0 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 10\sqrt{2} \approx 14.14$ m/s. A perfectly equal split — and as you'll see later, that symmetry is exactly why $45°$ gives maximum range.
Now let's pull them fully apart. A dividing line splits the screen. On the left, the full 2D parabola stays — dimmed but intact. On the right, two separate tracks appear, one for each component.

Look at the top-right strip — the horizontal track. The dot glides right at perfectly constant speed. Notice those equally spaced tick marks: equal distances in equal times. No force, no acceleration. Just steady, unwavering motion.

Now the vertical strip. The dot rises, decelerates, hangs for a moment at the peak, then falls back down — exactly like a ball tossed straight up. This is just gravity doing its simple, one-dimensional work.
Two simple problems, happening simultaneously. The intimidating parabola on the left is nothing more than these two motions running in parallel — a disguise hiding two very familiar ideas.

card.title: The Disguise Revealed

content.value: \text{Vertical: } y(t) = v_{0y}\, t - \tfrac{1}{2}g t^2 = 10\sqrt{2}\; t - 4.9\, t^2
Next: find how long the ball stays in the air using the vertical equation alone.
